Active versus Passive Policies One choice that must be confronted by all investors, individual as well as institutional, is the degree to which the portfolio will be actively versus passively managed. Recall that passive management is based on the belief that security prices usually are close to “fair” levels. Instead of spending time and other resources attempting to “beat the market,” that is, to find mis-priced securities with unusually attractive risk-r eturn characteristics, the investor simply assumes that she will be fairly compensated for the risk she is willing to take on and selects a portfolio consistent with her risk tolerance. 2 Passive management styles can be applied to both the security selection and the asset allo- cation decisions.

io consistent with her risk tolerance. 2 Passive management styles can be applied to both the security selection and the asset allo- cation decisions. With regard to asset allocation, passive management simply means that the manager does not depart from his or her “normal” asset-class weightings in response to chang-ing expectations about the performance of different markets. Those “normal” weights are based on the investor’s risk and return objectives, as discussed earlier. For example, an asset allocation for a 45-year-old investor of 65% equity, 20% bonds, and 15% cash equivalents would be considered fairly conventional. A purely passive manager would not depart from these weights in response to forecasts of market performances. The weighting scheme would be adjusted only in response to changes in r isk tolerance as age and wealth change over time. Next consider passive security selection. Imagine that you must choose a portfolio of stocks without access to any special information about security values. This would be the case if you believed that anything you know about a stock is already known by the rest of the investors in the market and therefore is already reflected in the stock price. If you cannot predict which stocks will be winners, you should broadly diversify your portfolio to avoid putting all your eggs in one basket. A natural course of action for such an investor would be to choose a port- folio with “a little bit of everything.” This reasoning leads one to look for a portf olio that is invested across the entire security market. We saw in Chapter 4 that some mutual fund operators have established index funds that follow just such a strategy. These funds hold each stock or bond in proportion to its rep-resentation in a particular index, such as the Standard & Poor’s 500 stock price index or the Barclays Capital (formerly the Lehman Brothers) Aggregate Bond Index.

n a particular index, such as the Standard & Poor’s 500 stock price index or the Barclays Capital (formerly the Lehman Brothers) Aggregate Bond Index. Holding an indexed portfolio represents purely passive security selection since the investor’s return simply dupli-cates the return of the overall market without making a bet on one or another stock or sector of the market. In contrast to passive strategies, active management assumes an ability to outguess other investors and to identify either securities or asset classes that will shine in the near future. Active security selection for institutional investors typically requires two layers: security analy-sis and portfolio choice. Security analysts specialize in particular industries and companies and prepare assessments of their particular market niches. The portfolio managers then sift through the reports of many analysts. They use forecasts of market conditions to make asset allocation decisions and use the security analysts’ recommendations to choose the particular securities to include within each asset class. The choice between active and passive strategies need not be all-or-nothing. One can pur- sue both active security selection and passive asset allocation, for example. In this case, the manager would maintain fixed asset allocation targ ets but would actively choose the securities 2 We discussed arguments for passive management in previous chapters.   09/08/12 5:21 PM 09/08/12 5:21 PM 728 Part SIX Active Investment Management within each asset class. Or one could pursue active asset allocation and passive security selec- tion. In this case, the manager might actively shift the allocation between equity and bond components of the portfolio but hold indexed por tfolios within each sector. Another mixed approach is called a passive core strategy. In this case, the manager indexes part of the portfolio, the passive core, and actively manages the rest of the portfolio.

ed a passive core strategy. In this case, the manager indexes part of the portfolio, the passive core, and actively manages the rest of the portfolio. Is active or passive management the better approach? It might seem at first blush that active managers have the edge because a ctive management is necessary to achieve outstanding performance. But remember that active managers start out with some disadvantages as well. They incur significant costs when preparing their analyses of markets and securities and incur heavier trading costs from the more rapid turnov er of their portfolios. If they don’t uncover information or insights currently unavailable to other investors (not a trivial task in a nearly efficient market), then all of this costly activity will be wasted, and they will underperform a passive strategy. In fact, low-cost passive strategies have performed surprisingly well in the last few decades, as we saw in Chapters 4 and 8. MONITORING AND REVISING INVESTMENT PORTFOLIOS Choosing the investment portfolio requires the in vestor to set objectives, acknowledge con- straints, determine asset-class proportions, and perform security analysis. Is the process ever finished and behind us? By the time we have completed all of these steps, many of the inputs we have used will be out of date. Moreover, our circumstances as well as our objectives change over time. Therefore, the investment process requires that we continually monitor and update our portfolios. This is the task of rebalancing, part of the feedback process described earlier in Table 22.1 and Figure 22.1 . Moreover, even when circumstances do not change, our portfolios necessarily will. Suppose you currently hold 1,000 shares of ExxonMobil, selling at $85 a share, and 1,000 shares of Microsoft, selling at $30. If the price of ExxonMobil falls to $60 a share, while that of Micro-soft rises to $35, the fractions of your portf olio allocated to each security change without your taking any direct action.

hare, while that of Micro-soft rises to $35, the fractions of your portf olio allocated to each security change without your taking any direct action. The value of your in vestment in ExxonMobil is now lower, and the value of the Microsoft investment is higher. Unless you are happy with this reallocation of investment proportions, you will need to take so me action to restore the portfolio weights to desired levels. Asset allocation also will change over time , as the investment performance of different asset classes diverges. If the stock market outperforms the bond market, the proportion of your portfolio invested in stocks will increase while the proportion invested in bonds will decrease. If you are uncomfortable with this shift in the asset mix, you must rebalance the portfolio by selling some of the stocks and purchasing bonds. Therefore, investing is a dynamic process, meaning that you must continually update and reevaluate your decisions over time. 22.5 Classify the following statements according to where each fits in the objective-constraints- policies framework. a. What else should investors review on a periodic basis in addition to asset allocation?   09/08/12 5:21 PM 09/08/12 5:21 PM www.mhhe.com/bkm734 Part SIX Active Investment Management 22.1 A convenient and effective way to organize the answer to this question is to cast it in the context of the investment policy statement framework. Risk: Endowment funds have no “safety nets” such as pension funds enjoy in the event of difficulty, either in the form of corporate assets to fall back on or a call on public assistance, such as from the Pension Benefit Guaranty Corporation. More-over, endowment fund cash flows may be highly erratic due to the uncertain tim-ing of income from gifts and/or bequests, while pension fund cash flows tend to be very predictable and steady. These differences suggest the typical endowment fund will adopt a more conservative risk-bearing posture than will the typical pension fund, both to asset-class exposures and to the type of security content of such exposures. Return: Because investment-related spending usually is limited to “income yield,” endowment funds often focus their return goals on the matter of current spendable income; pension funds, on the other hand, tend to adopt total return approaches, at least until a plan matures. Although inflation protection should be of great impor-tance to both types of funds, endowment funds appear to be less concerned with real return production than are pension funds, perhaps because of their common emphasis on “income now” in setting return goals. Time horizon: Theoretically, an endowment fund is a perpetuity, while a pension fund may well have a finite life span. Therefore, an endowment fund should operate with a very long-term view of investment.

rpetuity, while a pension fund may well have a finite life span. Therefore, an endowment fund should operate with a very long-term view of investment. However, such funds in practice tend to adopt shorter horizons than are typical of pension funds (just as they typically assume less risk). Their tendency to emphasize income production in the near term is the probable reason for this common occurrence. Liquidity: Endowment funds, particularly those that use gifts and bequests to sup- plement their investment income, often have fairly large liquidity reserves—both to protect against fluctuations in their cash flows and to reflect their generally conser-vative outlooks—while, except for very mature plans, pension funds tend to require minimum liquidity reserves. Endowment funds also frequently maintain substantial liquid holdings to provide for known future cash payout requirements, such as for new buildings. Taxes: Here, although differing in detail, the situations of the two forms of institu- tions are very much the same. In the United States, tax considerations are normally of minimal importance in both cases. Regulatory/legal: Endowment fund investment is carried out under state gov- ernance, while pension fund investment, in the United States, is carried out under federal law, specifically under ERISA. The difference is significant. Endowment funds operate under the prudent investor rule, whereby each investment must be judged on its own merits apart from any other portfolio holdings, while pension plans operate under a broader context for investment—each security being judged in terms of the portfolio as a whole—and an ERISA-mandated diversification requirement that often leads to wider asset-class exposures.

y being judged in terms of the portfolio as a whole—and an ERISA-mandated diversification requirement that often leads to wider asset-class exposures. Unique circumstances: Endowment funds often are faced with unique situations that sometimes affect pension fund management, including the scrutiny of such special-interest groups as trustees, alumni, faculty, student organizations, local community pressure groups, etc., each with separate and often incompatible con-straints and goals that may need to be accommodated in policy setting and/or in investment content. Similarly, endowment funds may be subjected to severe “social pressures” that, as in the case of tobacco firm divestment, can have an important investment impact by restricting the available universe of investment securities, mandating participation or nonparticipation in certain industries, sectors, or countries, or otherwise changing investment action from what it would SOLUTIONS TO CONCEPT checks   09/08/12 5:21 PM 09/08/12 5:21 PM www.mhhe.com/bkm Chapter 22 Investors and the Investment Process 735 otherwise have been. [FINA3101 lecture_1_v2] Today's Agenda Agenda Part I. Basic concepts about financial markets\\ Part II. Financial Asset Categories\\ Part III. Stock market indices\\ lightnavy Why do Investments Matter? 1. Basic Concepts in Financial Markets Why does Finance Exist? Finance exists because: Some people have innovative ideas but lack the funds to pursue them. Others possess financial resources but do not have new ideas to invest in. Entrepreneurs identify unique opportunities, such as: Developing new inventions, Meeting emerging market demands, Addressing shortages or unmet needs. 1. Basic Concepts in Financial Markets Saving to Investment [width=0.9]saving_to_invest.png 1. Basic Concepts in Financial Markets Example: Self-sufficient economy Without a financial system, new ventures can only be funded by the business owner's existing capital. Suppose we have 10 small businesses in an economy: 2 businesses are well-established, each with \100,000 in capital. 8 businesses are new startups, each with \10,000 in capital. Suppose that 20\ Innovative projects require an investment of \50,000 and create \150,000 in value. 1 established business and 1 new startup have an innovative project. 1.

[FINA3101 lecture_1_v2] ach with \10,000 in capital. Suppose that 20\ Innovative projects require an investment of \50,000 and create \150,000 in value. 1 established business and 1 new startup have an innovative project. 1. Basic Concepts in Financial Markets Example: Self-sufficient economy What is the total existing capital in this economy? Established Businesses: 2 businesses × \100,000/business = \200,000 New Startups: 8 businesses × \10,000/business = \80,000 Total Existing Capital: \200,000 + \80,000 = \280,000 How many innovative projects can be funded by individual businesses? Each innovative project requires an investment of \50,000. The established business, with \100,000 in capital, can individually fund their innovative projects. The new startup, with \10,000 in capital, cannot fund the project on its own. 1. Basic Concepts in Financial Markets Example: Self-sufficient economy What is the potential growth from these projects? Initial Total Capital: \280,000 Capital Invested in Funded Projects: 1 × \50,000 = \50,000 Value Created by Funded Projects: 1 × \150,000 = \150,000 Capital Remaining (Uninvested): \280,000 - \50,000 = \230,000 Total Wealth After Funded Projects: \230,000 + \150,000 = \380,000 Increase in Wealth: \380,000 - \280,000 = \100,000 1. Basic Concepts in Financial Markets Example: Isolated Businesses Now suppose there is a mechanism for businesses to pool their capital to fund projects.

[FINA3101 lecture_1_v2] se in Wealth: \380,000 - \280,000 = \100,000 1. Basic Concepts in Financial Markets Example: Isolated Businesses Now suppose there is a mechanism for businesses to pool their capital to fund projects. How many innovative projects can be funded? What is the potential growth rate of this economy with pooled investments? Initial Total Capital: \280,000 Capital Invested in Funded Projects: 2 × \50,000 = \100,000 Value Created by Funded Projects: 2 × \150,000 = \300,000 Capital Remaining (Uninvested): \280,000 - \100,000 = \180,000 Total Wealth After Funded Projects: \180,000 + \300,000 = \480,000 Increase in Wealth: \480,000 - \280,000 = \200,000 Basic Concepts in Financial Markets 1. Basic Concepts in Financial Markets What is an investment? An investment is the act of committing current resources with the expectation of generating a future return or benefits. A willingness to give up resources now such as time, money, or effort... With the hope that this sacrifice will lead to greater value or benefits later. 1. Basic Concepts in Financial Markets Key Investment Decisions Information asymmetry: How can we assess project quality and viability? How do we ensure entrepreneur commitment and performance? Liquidity: Projects take time to complete. What happens if we require early withdrawals? Risk: How do we manage exposure when funding uncertain outcomes? What strategies help achieve proper diversification? 1.

[FINA3101 lecture_1_v2] : Projects take time to complete. What happens if we require early withdrawals? Risk: How do we manage exposure when funding uncertain outcomes? What strategies help achieve proper diversification? 1. Basic Concepts in Financial Markets Information Asymmetry The investor and the entrepreneur/firm/other investors do not have the same information. The investor does not know the true quality of the project. What if the firm lies? The investor does not know how hard the entrepreneur will work. What if the entrepreneur takes my money and does nothing? How can I get her to work hard? If I want to sell my investment, what if the buyer doesn’t have the same information as me? If I say my investment is worth \100, how will they believe me? 1. Basic Concepts in Financial Markets Liquidity Projects take a long time to complete, but people might need their money back earlier. Banks solve this problem by pooling deposits together. Deposits can be redeemed at any time, but bank loans are long term. Markets solve this problem with tradeable securities. A company might issue equity (stocks) or a 10-year bond, but these shares can be traded immediately. But, having tradeable shares require symmetric information. 1. Basic Concepts in Financial Markets Risk Projects are risky and investors may want a way to reduce risk. Banks and funds solve this problem by pooling. Banks pool deposits from thousands of households and make loans to hundreds of firms.

[FINA3101 lecture_1_v2] isk Projects are risky and investors may want a way to reduce risk. Banks and funds solve this problem by pooling. Banks pool deposits from thousands of households and make loans to hundreds of firms. Markets solve this problem with securitization. A large equity or debt issuance is divided into millions of small, equal shares. An investor can hold only a small piece of the equity or debt. Investors can diversify themselves. 1. Basic Concepts in Financial Markets Investment can be general Is saving an investment? 1. Basic Concepts in Financial Markets Investment can be general Is saving an investment? You save a portion of the money that your parents endow you with You don't spend them -- you sacrifice a nice dinner in the restaurant Benefit from consuming it in the future (plus interest earned) the value of buying a new laptop at cheaper price during year-end sales The trade-off between saving and consumption has profound implications for both financial markets and the broader economy. Financial vs. Real Assets 1.1. Financial vs. Real Assets Real assets: assets that you can directly use for the production of goods or services (e.g., Land, machines, customer relationships, or IP). Financial assets: means or contracts by which an individual holds claims on real assets (e.g., stocks, bonds, CDs). Questions: Are US dollar bills financial or real assets? Is a patent for a new invention a financial or real asset? 1.1. Financial vs.

Financial vs. Real Assets Types of Financial Assets Financial assets are typically grouped into three main types: Fixed-Income Securities:\\ These instruments offer a predictable stream of income -- either a fixed amount or one determined by a predefined formula. Bonds are the most common example. Equity Securities:\\ Represent ownership in a company. Investors holding these assets may receive dividends and have voting rights. Stocks are the primary example. Derivative Securities:\\ These are financial contracts whose value is based on the performance of an underlying asset, index, or rate. Common examples include options and forward contracts. Financial Markets 1.2. Financial Markets Competitive Markets Financial markets are very competitive. In competitive financial markets, higher returns come with higher risk. Market competitiveness leads to efficient pricing of securities. Put simply: there's no such thing as a free lunch (i.e., you can't earn excess returns without taking on additional risk). 1.2. Financial Markets Competitive Markets For Bond Alpha, its current price (P_α,0) is \980 and its value at maturity (P_α,1) in one year is \1000. For Bond Beta, its current price (P_β,0) is \900 and its value at maturity (P_β,1) in one year is \950.

[FINA3101 lecture_1_v2] Alpha, its current price (P_α,0) is \980 and its value at maturity (P_α,1) in one year is \1000. For Bond Beta, its current price (P_β,0) is \900 and its value at maturity (P_β,1) in one year is \950. Thus, the return for Bond Alpha (R_α) is 1000/980 ≈ 1.0204, which is less than the return for Bond Beta (R_β) of 950/900 ≈ 1.0556. So, R_α < R_β. 1.2. Financial Markets Competitive Markets For Bond Alpha, its current price (P_α,0) is \980 and its value at maturity (P_α,1) in one year is \1000. For Bond Beta, its current price (P_β,0) is \900 and its value at maturity (P_β,1) in one year is \950. Thus, the return for Bond Alpha (R_α) is 1000/980 ≈ 1.0204, which is less than the return for Bond Beta (R_β) of 950/900 ≈ 1.0556. So, R_α < R_β. People will want to **sell Bond Alpha** and **buy Bond Beta**. The current price of Bond Alpha (P_α,0) will **decrease** (). The current price of Bond Beta (P_β,0) will **increase** (). 1.2. Financial Markets Competitive Markets Returns are inversely related to current prices: R_i=P_i,1P_i,0. As P_α,0 decreases, the return of Bond Alpha will **increase** (R_α ). As P_β,0 increases, the return of Bond Beta will **decrease** (R_β ). This process continues until the returns (or yields) of Bond Alpha and Bond Beta are equal, R_α=R_β, reflecting the efficiency of the competitive market. 1.2.

Financial Markets Key Participants Firms: These are typically the net demanders of capital, meaning they need to borrow money to fund their operations and growth. Households: Often the net suppliers of capital, households save money and make it available for others to invest. Governments: Governments can act as both borrowers and lenders depending on their fiscal situation. 1.2. Financial Markets Important Participants Beyond Main Players Financial Intermediaries: These include banks, investment companies, and insurance companies. They act as bridges, connecting those who have capital (suppliers) with those who need it (demanders). Investment Banks: These institutions offer specialized services that help firms raise capital, leveraging their expertise and economies of scale. Venture Capital (VC) and Private Equity (PE) Firms: They provide funding directly to companies not listed on public stock exchanges, often focusing on start-ups and private businesses. Fintech Companies: These firms use technological innovation to offer new financial products and services, revolutionizing how finance operates. lightnavy Part 2: Financial asset categories Three main financial asset categories: Fixed-income Equity Derivatives Fixed-income securities 2.1.

The higher the risk of a borrower defaulting, the higher the interest rate lenders will demand on that bond. 2.1. Fixed-income securities Inflation Risk Inflation Risk: Consider a 10-year bond with a \1 million face value and a fixed annual coupon of 2\ When inflation rises, the purchasing power of those coupon payments declines. Investors are primarily concerned with returns in real (inflation-adjusted) terms. Therefore, the greater the inflation risk, the higher the bond’s interest rate must be to compensate investors. 2.1. Equity Markets The equity market is a marketplace where investors buy and sell ownership shares (called stocks or equities) in publicly traded companies. It serves as a platform for companies to raise capital and for investors to share in company profits and growth. Main Functions Primary Market: where new stocks are issued through initial public offerings (IPOs). Companies raise funds directly from investors. Secondary Market: where existing shares are traded among investors. Companies do not receive money from these transactions. Almost 1 million shares of Hershey traded on this day. 2.3.2. Common Stocks Concept: Common Stocks: The term "common" indicates that each share grants its holder one vote in corporate decisions. Voting Rights: Unlike common shares, some other stock types may grant multiple votes per share. Limited Liability: Shareholders are only responsible for losses up to the amount they invested -- they are not personally liable for the company’s debts. Perpetual Life: Common stock has no maturity date; it remains valid as long as the company exists. Residual Claims: Shareholders are entitled to any remaining profits after all other obligations have been met. Dividends: Company earnings may be distributed to shareholders as cash dividends or reinvested back into the business. 2.3.3. Corporate Governance and Voting Corporations as Democracies Representative Democracy Analogy: In parliamentary systems, citizens vote for members of parliament, who then choose the executive branch (the government). Similarly, in corporations, shareholders vote for board members, who in turn appoint the executive management team. Direct Democracy Element: Corporations also share features of direct democracy -- shareholders can directly submit proposals advocating for changes or reforms within the company.

Derivatives Financial contracts (e.g. options, future, swap) whose payoffs depend on value of another asset, the underlying asset (commodity prices, stock prices, etc.) or a future contingency (e.g. a default event). Risk: they are generally riskier than traditional financial assets (e.g. stocks, bonds). Return: Any gain for one party in the contract comes directly from a loss by the other party. Primary Market Definition The Primary Market is where companies initially issue their securities to investors. Three types of primary market issuances: Initial Public Offerings (IPOs): The process by which a privately-held company transitions to a public entity by offering its shares for the first time on a public exchange to secure funding. We focus on these issuances Seasoned Equity Offerings (SEOs): Already publicly-traded companies raise further capital by issuing and selling new shares to the public. The Private Market: Comprises companies whose ownership shares are not listed or traded on a public stock exchange. 1.1.

Engages various stakeholders: current and future shareholders, regulators, investment banks, etc. 1.1. The IPO process Investment bank hiring Shelf-registration Bookbuilding Prospectus issuance and SEC approval Public trading 1.1. The IPO process 1. Investment bank hiring: The IPO companies enlist one or more investment banks. Examples include Goldman Sachs, JP Morgan, Morgan Stanley, and UBS. These financial institutions are known as underwriters. 1.1. The IPO process 2. Shelf-registration: The firm registers with the SEC and prepares a preliminary prospectus (red-herring document). Note: Companies can register more shares than initially offered in the IPO, allowing for gradual sale over the next three years. 1.1. The IPO process 3. Bookbuilding: Investment bankers conduct roadshows to introduce the firm to potential investors. Prospective investors then indicate their interest by specifying desired quantity and price. Underwriters compile these investor interests into a "book," adjusting the stock's final quantity and price accordingly. 1.1. The IPO process 4.

Shares priced at \38, one of the largest tech offerings ever. Massive public excitement – retail investors expected a first-day “pop.” Example: Uma Swaminathan invested nearly half her life savings. Question: Why do investors often expect IPOs to soar on the first day? 1.2. The case of Facebook's IPO Information Asymmetry Question: What was the information asymmetry in this IPO? Facebook lowered revenue projections just before the IPO. Banks selectively disclosed this to institutional clients. Retail investors had little or no access to this information. Hedge funds shorted the stock, while individuals like Uma Swaminathan lost savings. Question: Should all investors have access to the same information before an IPO? What are the consequences of selective disclosure? 1.2.

NASDAQ’s trading system failed on opening day. Delays and technical errors prevented some cancellations and orders. Morgan Stanley intervened with a ``greenshoe option'' to stabilize the price. Stock closed flat on day one, then quickly declined. 1.2. The case of Facebook's IPO Aftermath \& Lessons Question: How much market value had Facebook lost since the IPO? Who were affected? Retail investors lost billions; institutions profited. Morgan Stanley paid fines for breaching the ``Chinese Wall.'' By August 2012, Facebook lost \~\50 billion in value. Long-term: Facebook rebounded and became one of the most valuable companies globally. Question: What lessons should investors learn from Facebook’s IPO flop? How should regulators respond? The Secondary Market The secondary market facilitates the buying and selling of previously issued securities among investors. We will discuss three examples: The New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) The National Association of Securities Dealers Automated Quotations (NASDAQ) Electronic Communication Networks (ECNs) 2.1. Types of Markets Four different types of markets: Direct Search Markets Brokered Markets Dealers Markets Auction Markets 2.1.1. Direct search markets 1. Direct Search Markets: These are the least organized market structures. Participants (buyers and sellers) must actively seek each other out. Example: Facebook Marketplace 2.1.2. Brokered markets 2. Brokered Markets: Represent a step up in organization from direct search.

[FINA3101 Lecture_2_spring_2026] Participants (buyers and sellers) must actively seek each other out. Example: Facebook Marketplace 2.1.2. Brokered markets 2. Brokered Markets: Represent a step up in organization from direct search. Here, a broker's primary function is to efficiently bring together interested buyers and sellers. Example: real estate brokers or insurance brokers 2.1.3. Dealers markets 3. Dealer Markets: More structured than brokered markets, requiring higher trading volume. Dealers buy and hold assets, aiming to resell them at a profit (premium). This profit is known as the bid-ask spread. The bid price is what the dealer pays to acquire the asset. The ask price is what the dealer charges to sell the asset. A key advantage is reduced search costs for participants. Common examples include most Over-the-Counter (OTC) bond markets and foreign exchange markets. 2.1.4. Auction markets 4. Auction Markets: These are the most integrated market structures. All participants converge in a single location, which can be physical or electronic. Advantage: Best prices are found efficiently through mutual agreement, eliminating the bid-ask spread. Example: The New York Stock Exchange (NYSE). 2.2. Types of Orders There are two primary categories of trading orders: Market Orders: Instructions to buy or sell immediately at the best available current market price.

[FINA3101 Lecture_2_spring_2026] York Stock Exchange (NYSE). 2.2. Types of Orders There are two primary categories of trading orders: Market Orders: Instructions to buy or sell immediately at the best available current market price. Price-Contingent Orders: Orders where investors set a specific price at which they are willing to trade.\\ Limit Buy Order: An order to purchase if the asset's price falls to or below a set level. Limit Sell Order: An order to sell if the asset's price rises to or above a set level. Limit Order Book: A compiled list of unexecuted limit orders awaiting specific price conditions. 2.3. Trading Mechanisms How Orders Are Executed When an investor places an order, brokers can execute the trade through three main mechanisms: Over-The-Counter (OTC) Dealer Markets Electronic Communication Networks (ECNs) Designated Market Maker (DMM) Markets 2.3.1. Over-The-Counter Dealer Market 1. Over-The-Counter (OTC) Dealer Markets: Brokers register with the SEC as securities dealers. Dealers publicly quote prices at which they are willing to buy (bid) or sell (ask) securities. A broker then contacts the dealer offering the most favorable price to execute a trade. 2.3.2. Electronic Communication Networks 2. Electronic Communication Networks (ECNs): These are the most prevalent trading mechanisms today. All traders can directly submit their limit and market orders onto a computerized network. The ECN automatically matches and executes buy and sell orders without broker intervention.

[FINA3101 Lecture_2_spring_2026] mechanisms today. All traders can directly submit their limit and market orders onto a computerized network. The ECN automatically matches and executes buy and sell orders without broker intervention. ECN subscribers are typically large traders, such as institutional investors and other broker-dealers. Advantages: High anonymity, high speed, and low cost. 2.3.3. Designated Market Makers Markets 3. Designated Market Maker (DMM) Markets: A Market Maker is a trader who publicly quotes both a bid and an ask price for a security. Market makers play a crucial role in providing liquidity to the market. Designated Market Makers (DMMs) are specific market makers committed to supplying market liquidity using their own capital. DMMs are expected to actively reduce large bid-ask spreads by trading from their own inventory. 2.4. The NYSE Size: As of April 2024, the NYSE is recognized as the largest stock exchange globally, with a total market value nearing \27 trillion. Market Structure: It functions as an auction market, centralizing buy and sell orders to foster transparent price determination through direct competition among participants. Electronic Trading with DMM Support: The NYSE largely relies on electronic trading systems for efficiency. However, Designated Market Makers (DMMs) play an essential role during turbulent market conditions, stepping in to provide stability and ensure continuous liquidity.

[FINA3101 Lecture_2_spring_2026] ic trading systems for efficiency. However, Designated Market Makers (DMMs) play an essential role during turbulent market conditions, stepping in to provide stability and ensure continuous liquidity. Consolidated Pricing: Prices on the NYSE are a direct aggregation of all buyer and seller interest, ensuring that the executed price reflects the collective market consensus at any given moment. 2.4. The NASDAQ Size: NASDAQ stands as the world's second-largest stock exchange, with a market capitalization of approximately \25 trillion as of April 2024. It is particularly known for listing a high concentration of technology and growth-oriented companies. Market Structure: Unlike the NYSE's auction model, NASDAQ operates as a dealer market. This means that trading occurs through a network of market makers who quote prices (both bid and ask) and stand ready to buy from or sell to investors, using their own inventory. Extensive Network of Market Makers: Over a thousand market makers are active on NASDAQ. Crucially, for many listed stocks, there are multiple market makers competing to facilitate trades. Competitive Bid-Ask Spreads: The presence of numerous competing market makers for a single stock directly contributes to narrower bid-ask spreads. 2.4. The Electronic Communication Networks ECNs provide brokers with highly efficient, fully automated order matching systems. Traders directly submit their orders to a centralized limit order book.

[FINA3101 Lecture_2_spring_2026] . 2.4. The Electronic Communication Networks ECNs provide brokers with highly efficient, fully automated order matching systems. Traders directly submit their orders to a centralized limit order book. All submitted orders are matched and executed automatically by the network. These private networks distinguish themselves by competing fiercely on latency, which is the speed at which trading orders are placed and processed. The very brief discrepancies that can appear across different ECNs create opportunities for high-frequency traders to engage in profitable arbitrage strategies. 2.5. Trading Costs and new trading strategies Trading involves three primary types of costs: Brokerage Fees: Vary significantly based on the level of service provided (e.g., full-service financial consulting vs. basic execution, custody, or margin loans). Dealer Bid-Ask Spreads: The difference between the dealer's buying and selling prices. Price Impact: Large orders can significantly influence a security's demand/supply balance, thus affecting its price. This is particularly relevant for large institutional investors. Strategies to Minimize Trading Costs: Use limit orders instead of current orders Split large orders into several smaller ones 2.6. New Trading Strategies Algorithmic Trading: Involves using computer programs to make trading decisions. Accounts for over half of all transactions on US stock exchanges. High-Frequency Trading (HFT): Closely related to algorithmic trading.

[FINA3101 Lecture_2_spring_2026] ing: Involves using computer programs to make trading decisions. Accounts for over half of all transactions on US stock exchanges. High-Frequency Trading (HFT): Closely related to algorithmic trading. Characterized by intense competition for very small profits. Requires extremely high speed, making physical location critical (latency matters). Dark Pools: Trading venues where participants remain anonymous and order books are not publicly disclosed. Beneficial for executing large block trades to minimize price impact (e.g., as seen in the Barclays scandal). Trading on Margin lightnavy\\ 3. Buying on Margin [width=0.9]margin.png\\ 3.1. Buying on Margin The investment approach where you use borrowed funds to acquire securities is termed Buying on Margin. The Margin represents the portion of your total investment that is funded by your own equity. Initial Margin = (Equity at time t)/(Total Investment at time t) This is specifically the Initial Margin, calculated at the moment of the investment. After the price increase (in period t+1), the margin percentage adjusts to: Margin_t+1 = Equity_t+1Assets_t+1 3.1. Buying on Margin Initial Margin: The percentage of a security's purchase price an investor must pay upfront. The Federal Reserve Board sets this ratio, which must be at least 50\ Maintenance Margin: The minimum equity percentage required in a margin account after the initial purchase. Exchanges or brokerage firms set this, typically between 30\ Margin Call: A demand for additional funds or securities when an account's margin falls below the maintenance margin. Failing to meet a margin call often leads to forced liquidation of positions. 3.1. Buying on Margin The Margin Protects the Broker When a broker lends to an investor, the broker has exposure to the risk of the asset. The size of the margin will depend on the riskiness of the assets. A risky stock might have a margin of around 50\ A safe government bond might have a margin of around 5\ 3.1. Selling on Margin 3.2. Selling on Margin (Short Sales) Concept: Short selling involves borrowing a security and immediately selling it at the current market price. When to short? When you anticipate a significant drop in the security’s price. Once the price declines, you repurchase the security at the lower price and return it to the lender. Your profit equals the difference between the selling price and the repurchase price. Thus, you gain from the price decline. 3.2. Why Short Sell? Trading on Margin Price Spirals: The Risk of Forced Liquidation For a large buyer who buys on margin (Long Position): Price for some reason Margin Forced sale of shares for margin call Pushes price further Margin even more More forced sales... (A downward spiral or "death spiral").

[FINA3101 Lecture_2_spring_2026] who buys on margin (Long Position): Price for some reason Margin Forced sale of shares for margin call Pushes price further Margin even more More forced sales... (A downward spiral or "death spiral"). For a large short-seller (Short Position): Price for some reason Margin Forced buy-back of shares for margin call ("short squeeze") Pushes price further Margin even more More forced buy-backs... (An upward spiral or "gamma squeeze"). A seemingly small initial price disturbance can lead to a huge run-up or a severe crash due to these feedback loops of forced liquidations. 3. Trading on Margin Margin Calls: Archegos Capital Management [width=0.8]archegos.png 3. Trading on Margin Archegos Capital Management Overview On March 26, 2021, Archegos failed to meet margin calls issued by at least eight major banks. Archegos is a family office established in 2012 by Bill Hwang. Hwang previously worked as a hedge fund manager at Tiger Management. In 2012, he faced insider trading charges related to Chinese banking stocks. Archegos was launched with \200 million and had grown to \10 billion by 2021, reflecting an approximate annual return of 54\ 3. Trading on Margin "Secret" to Archegos Success Archegos operated under the radar by avoiding direct stock ownership, which allowed it to bypass public disclosure requirements in the U.S. Its trading strategy relied heavily on total return swaps. A swap exchanges fixed payments for floating returns on a notional principal.

[FINA3101 Lecture_2_spring_2026] allowed it to bypass public disclosure requirements in the U.S. Its trading strategy relied heavily on total return swaps. A swap exchanges fixed payments for floating returns on a notional principal. Long: Receive stock returns (price increase + dividend), pay fees. Short: Receive fees, pay stock returns (price increase + dividend). Banks required margin (collateral) for these transactions, but it typically amounted to just 5–20\ The key to Archegos's performance was leverage—its positions were commonly leveraged up to 8 times. 3. Trading on Margin Archegos had large levered positions in US and Chinese TMT stocks, including ViacomCBS and Baidu. [width=0.9]bigmoo.png 3. Trading on Margin Archegos Margin Call Default As paper losses piled up on Archegos’ long-levered swap positions, prime brokers asked for more collateral. When Archegos defaulted, GS and MS began liquidating \19B of positions, this led to a fire-sale. This started a race to liquidate positions, with GS and MS leading the way. Some banks lagged behind and sold the positions at depressed prices. Nomura suffered a \3B loss. Credit Suisse suffered a \$5.5B loss that partly contributed to its demise in 2023. 3. Trading on Margin Bank Losses from Archegos Fallout [width=0.9]nomura.png 3. Trading on Margin Bank Losses from Archegos Fallout Large brokers had a correlated exposure to Archegos’s trades. The liquidation of Archegos’s trades led to a contagion effect on other broker’s balance sheets.

[FINA3101 Lecture_2_spring_2026] Margin Bank Losses from Archegos Fallout Large brokers had a correlated exposure to Archegos’s trades. The liquidation of Archegos’s trades led to a contagion effect on other broker’s balance sheets. If one fund can cause this much harm to the balance sheet of banks, what happens if multiple funds collapse?

[FINA3101 Lecture_3] Why Returns and Risk matter? Core Objective: How to optimally construct an investment portfolio? The Trade-off: You cannot have higher expected returns without accepting higher risk. Decision Making: Without quantifying these two numbers, "investing" is just "gambling." The Goal: To construct the optimal portfolio, we need the return and risk metrics to evaluate its efficiency. Part 1.1. Multiple-Period Returns Holding Period Returns (HPR) The holding period return (HPR) is a key concept: Comprised of two components: capital gains (price change) and dividend yield If an investor holds a stock from day t to day t+1, the capital gain or loss is calculated as: Capital Gain_t,t+1 = P_t+1 - P_tP_t The dividend yield over the same period is: Dividend_t,t+1 = D_t+1P_t Part 1.1. Multiple-Period Returns To Yield or To Grow When is a Dividend-Heavy strategy your best friend? Using investment income to cover expenses. A ``sideways" or declining market. Lower income tax rate (0\ When should you chase Capital Gains instead? Long investment horizons. Higher tolerance for volatility. Capital gain tax is lower and can be deferred. Part 1.1. Expected Return and Risk HPRs are realized returns, which happened in the past. Stock return in the future is a random variable. Return's distribution matters for investment decisions. Chapters 5.4, 5.5 Part 2.1. Expected Return It is forward looking and takes into account of all states of the world. Definition of expected return: E(Ret) = sum_s (p(s) x Re(s)) where s indicates the state of the world, p(s) indicates the probability of state s happens, and Ret(s) indicates the stock’s return in state s. [FINA3101 Lecture_3] Why does anyone invest in Treasury bills? What aspects of Treasury bills do investors like? Part 2.2. Variance of Returns as a Measure of Risk Risk Matters Variance is the most common metric of risk. What does variance represent economically? Many investors love T-bills, which offers certain return for a certain holding period (e.g. one-month T-bill rate is certain for following one month). One-month T-bill’s following month return has zero variance. Stock’s following month return is a random variable and has nonzero variance. All else equal, investors prefer more certain payoffs. Part 2.2. Variance of Returns as a Measure of Risk Variance Calculation Definition of variance: Var(Ret) = sum_s \p(s) x (Ret(s) - E(Ret))^2 \ where s indicates the state of the world, p(s) indicates the probability of state s happens, and Ret(s) indicates the stock’s return in state s. Standard deviation: sqrt(Var(Ret)) Part 2.2. Variance of Returns as a Measure of Risk Variance Calculation Example lcc States & Probability & Return in this state \\ Very bad & 0.10 & -0.05 \\ Bad & 0.20 & 0.05 \\ Average & 0.40 & 0.15 \\ Good & 0.20 & 0.25 \\ Very good & 0.10 & 0.35 \\ Recall:E(R) = 0.15 Var(r) &= sum p(s) x (R(s) - E(R))^2 \\ &= [0.10 x (-0.05 - 0.15)^2 + 0.20 x (0.05 - 0.15)^2 \\ &+ 0.40 x (0.15 - 0.15)^2 + 0.2 x (0.25 - 0.15)^2 \\ &+ 0.10 x (0.35 - 0.15)^2] \\ &= 0.012 Part 2.2.

Other Measures of Risk Value at Risk (VaR) How much would you lose if the returns on your investment were in the x^th percentile of the return distribution? Usually x is taken to be small (e.g., 1\ The loss that you would face if the return was in the x^th percentile of the distribution is called the x^th-Value at Risk. We usually denote the Value at Risk, VaR. Remark: The value at risk is often expressed in terms of returns rather than loss. Part 2.3. Inflation and Interest Rates Expected Inflation and Interest Rates Investors should require higher nominal interest rates when inflation is higher. Irving Fisher (1930) proposed the following relation: r_nominal = r_real + E[i] But can investors forecast inflation? Irving Fisher also introduced the concept of money illusion. Under money illusion, human beings are not able to fully incorporate inflation. In other words, under money illusion, human beings think in nominal terms rather than real terms. Whether money illusion exists is still an ongoing debate.

[FINA3101 Lecture_4] This Lecture's Agenda Our goal in this lecture is to calculate an investor's optimal allocation to a risky portfolio of assets. Everyone is unique, each person feels differently about risk. How can we formally model people's feelings? What's a portfolio? How do we combine different risky assets? The risk free asset is special. It gives us returns without any risk. 1. Risk Aversion Why Expected Return is Not Enough The Question: Do investors make decisions based only on the average (expected) return? The Answer: No. Investors are generally Risk Averse. We value a "sure thing" more than a risky gamble with the same average payoff. To accept risk, investors demand a Risk Premium (extra return). 1.1. The St. Petersburg Paradox (Bernoulli, 1713) The Setup The Game Imagine a coin toss game where the pot starts at \2 and doubles every time heads appears. The game ends when tails appears. l c c Outcome & Probability & Payout \\ 1 Head & 1/2 & \2 \\ 2 Heads & 1/4 & \4 \\ 3 Heads & 1/8 & \8 \\ 4 Heads & 1/16 & \16 \\ Question: How much would you pay to play this game? 1.1. The St. Petersburg Paradox The Flaw of Expected Value Let's calculate the Expected Value (Average Payout): E[V] &= ((1)/(2) x 2) + ((1)/(4) x 4) + ((1)/(8) x 8) + \\ &= 1 + 1 + 1 + \\ &= (Infinite) The Conclusion The math says you should bet your life savings to play, because the average payout is Infinite. \\ Reality: No one pays that much. This proves that Expected Return is not enough to explain decisions.

[FINA3101 Lecture_4] The math says you should bet your life savings to play, because the average payout is Infinite. \\ Reality: No one pays that much. This proves that Expected Return is not enough to explain decisions. We care about Utility, not just dollars. 1.1. Utility Function The Origins of the Utility Function Gabriel Cramer (1728) In a letter to Bernoulli, Cramer noted that "mathematicians estimate money in proportion to its quantity, and men of good sense [estimate it] in proportion to the usage they make out of it." Daniel Bernoulli (1738) Nicolas's cousin, Daniel Bernoulli, proposed that a mathematical function should be used to modify the expected value. This is the origin of the Bernoulli utility function, u(w). 1.1. Utility Function Expected Utility: von Neumann-Morgenstern John von Neumann and Oskar Morgenstern formalized expected utility theory in 1947. Suppose there are n future states, and wealth w can take on values \w_1, w_2, , w_n\. The probability of each state is P = \p_1, p_2, , p_n\. [Expected Utility] The expected utility is the sum of probabilities and possible utilities: E(u(w)) = p_1u(w_1) + p_2u(w_2) + + p_nu(w_n) 1.2. Risk and Risk Aversion Utility functions are assumed to increase with wealth, but at a decreasing rate. u'(w) > 0 (More wealth is better) u''(w) < 0 (Diminishing marginal utility) Risk aversion follows directly from the concavity of u(w) and uncertainty. People dislike losses more than they like gains.

[FINA3101 Lecture_4] u'(w) > 0 (More wealth is better) u''(w) < 0 (Diminishing marginal utility) Risk aversion follows directly from the concavity of u(w) and uncertainty. People dislike losses more than they like gains. Therefore, they will need to be compensated for taking on risk. 1.2. Risk and Risk Aversion Utility Function \& Risk Aversion [width=0.7]utility_function.png 1.2. Risk and Risk Aversion Certainty Equivalent The certainty equivalent is the certain (risk-free) amount of wealth that gives the same utility as a risky portfolio. If w_c is the certainty equivalent of a risky portfolio with expected future wealth E(w_p), then: u(w_c) = E(u(w_p)) For a risk-averse investor, w_c < E(w_p). The difference, E(w_p) - w_c, is the risk premium. 1.2. Risk and Risk Aversion Investor Types Risk Averse Exhibits decreasing marginal utility. u'(w) > 0 and u''(w) < 0. These investors require a risk premium. Risk Neutral Does not exhibit decreasing marginal utility. u'(w) > 0 and u''(w) = 0. These investors do not require a risk premium. Risk Loving Exhibits increasing marginal utility. u'(w) > 0 and u''(w) > 0. These investors are willing to pay to take on more risk. 1.2. Risk and Risk Aversion What is A? (In Classic Investment) In classic investment, we assume investors are rational, meaning: They are risk averse, with A > 0. How to estimate risk aversion A? Use questionnaires. Observe individuals’ decisions when confronted with risk. Observe how much people are willing to pay to avoid risk. 1.2.

Powerball's Expected Return E(Ret) = sum_s (p(s) x Ret(s)) E(Total) &= E(Jackpot) + E(Non-Jackpot) \\ &= \0.1078 + \0.2688 \\ &= \0.3766 Net Expected Loss Expected Return: \0.38 Cost of Ticket: \2.00 Net Expected Value: \0.38 - \2.00 = -\1.62 This means that for every \2 ticket you buy with this jackpot and these assumptions, you can expect to lose \1.62 on average over the long run. lightnavy\\ 2.1. Mean-Variance Investors Utility Function We can assume a specific functional form for the expected utility function. For an investor investing in a portfolio with expected return E(r) and volatility σ: U = E(r) - (1)/(2)Aσ^2 U is the level of expected utility. A > 0 when investors are risk averse. A = 0 when investors are risk neutral. A < 0 when investors are risk loving. Each investor has a unique risk aversion coefficient, A, and chooses a portfolio to maximize their unique expected utility. 2.1. Mean-Variance Investors Mean-Variance Investor (A > 0) Investors love high expected returns but hate high risks. The metric for risks is variance. The mean-variance investor (A > 0): Given two portfolios that offer the same expected return, the investor will choose the one with lower variance.

[FINA3101 Lecture_4] t hate high risks. The metric for risks is variance. The mean-variance investor (A > 0): Given two portfolios that offer the same expected return, the investor will choose the one with lower variance. Given two portfolios that offer the same variance, the investor will choose the one with higher expected return. 2.1. Mean-Variance Investors Utility Function - Example An investor has a risk-aversion coefficient of A=3. She is choosing between two portfolios: Portfolio P: E(r_p) = 15\ Portfolio Q: E(r_q) = 10\ What is the utility of each portfolio? U_p &= E(r_p) - (1)/(2)Aσ_p^2 = 0.15 - (1)/(2)(3)(0.20^2) = 0.09 \\ U_q &= E(r_q) - (1)/(2)Aσ_q^2 = 0.10 - (1)/(2)(3)(0.12^2) = 0.0784 This investor will choose Portfolio P because U_p > U_q. 2.2. Mean-Variance Investors Indifference Curves An indifference curve plots combinations of risk (σ) and expected return (E(r)) that provide an investor with the same level of utility. Example An investor with risk aversion A = 2 is indifferent between two portfolios: Portfolio A: E(r_A) = 6\ Portfolio B: E(r_B) = 13\ Since the investor is indifferent, U_A = U_B: E(r_A) - (1)/(2)Aσ_A^2 &= E(r_B) - (1)/(2)Aσ_B^2 \\ 0.06 - (1)/(2)(2)(0.03^2) &= 0.13 - (1)/(2)(2)σ_B^2 \\ 0.0591 &= 0.13 - σ_B^2 \\ σ_B^2 &= 0.0709 σ_B = 0.266 = 26.6\ 2.2. Security B1 is strictly dominated by: ?? 2.2. Mean-Variance Investors Indifference Curve Graph ![width=]utility_2.png Capital Allocation lightnavy\\ 3.1. Capital Allocation Defining a Portfolio A portfolio describes what fraction of your wealth is invested in each stock or asset. We denote a portfolio by the vector w, where each w_i is the fraction of money invested in risky asset i. For n assets, the sum of the portfolio weights must equal one: sum_i=1^n w_i = 1 3.1. Capital Allocation Problem Statement How should an investor construct an optimal portfolio from million of assets available on the market?\\ Investors typically seek to balance ``Expected Return" against ``Volatility". The Markowitz Model (MPT) cleverly creates a road map for the process: Separate the investment universe into two distinct categories: a Risk-Free Asset and the portfolio of Only Risky Assets. Find the optimal combination of the Risk-Free Asset and the portfolio of Only Risky Assets. You either maximize expected return for a target volatility level, or minimize volatility for a target return. You construct the optimal portfolio of Only Risky Assets (covered in the next lecture) 3.2.

[FINA3101 Lecture_4] u either maximize expected return for a target volatility level, or minimize volatility for a target return. You construct the optimal portfolio of Only Risky Assets (covered in the next lecture) 3.2. Capital Allocation Capital Allocation Between Risk-Free and Risky Portfolios The allocation between a risk-free asset and a risky portfolio is called a complete portfolio. When a fraction y of wealth in risky portfolio p and 1-y in the risk-free portfolio, the complete portfolio is: w_C = (1-y)w_rf + y w_p If the risky portfolio is comprised of an equity fund and a bond fund: w_C = (1-y)w_rf+ y w_p,Ew_E + y w_p,Bw_B 3.2. Capital Allocation Realized Portfolio Return Example Stock X: price today = 100, weight w_X=0.75. Stock Y: price today = 10, weight w_Y=0.25. Next month: P_X=80, P_Y=24. Find realized return. R_1 = w_X P_X,1P_X,0 + w_Y P_Y,1P_Y,0 = 0.75 · (80)/(100) + 0.25 · (24)/(10) = 0.6 + 0.6 = 1.2 3.2. Capital Allocation Maximization Problem \[ _y \; U = y[E(R_p)-R_f] + R_f - 2A y^2σ_p^2 \] First-order condition: (dU)/(dy) = E(R_p)-R_f - Aσ_p^2 y = 0 3.4. Capital Allocation The Optimal Allocation y^* y^* = (E(R_p) - R_f)/(Aσ_p^2) This formula tells us that the optimal fraction of wealth invested in the risky portfolio is: Directly proportional to the risky portfolio's risk premium (E(R_p) - R_f). Inversely proportional to the investor's risk aversion (A). Inversely proportional to the variance of the risky portfolio (σ_p^2). 3.4. Capital Allocation Example: Optimal Allocation Suppose a risky portfolio has an expected return of 15\ E(R_p) = 0.15 σ_p = 0.22 R_f = 0.07 A = 4 The optimal allocation to the risky portfolio is: y^* &= (E(R_p) - R_f)/(Aσ_p^2) = (0.15 - 0.07)/(4 x (0.22)^2) \\ &= (0.08)/(4 x 0.0484) = (0.08)/(0.1936) ≈ 0.4132 The investor should invest 41.32\ 3.4. Capital Allocation The Optimal Complete Portfolio Once we have y^*, we can find the properties of the optimal complete portfolio, C*: Expected Return: E(R_C^*) = R_f + y^*[E(R_p) - R_f] Volatility: σ_C^* = y^*σ_p Continuing the previous example: E(R_C^*) &= 0.07 + 0.4132(0.15 - 0.07) = 10.31\ σ_C^* &= 0.4132 x 0.22 = 9.09\ 3.4. Capital Allocation The Capital Market Line (CML) When the risky portfolio is a market index (like the S\&P 500), the resulting CAL is called the Capital Market Line (CML). The CML represents the risk-return combinations of a passive investment strategy. It's a special case of the CAL where the risky asset is the market portfolio. [Capital Market Line Equation] E(R_C) = R_f + ((E(R_M) - R_f)/(σ_M))σ_C Where M denotes the market portfolio. 3.4.

Conclusion Every investor is different and demands a unique risk-reward trade-off. We can model this trade-off using a utility function. The risk-free asset is special. Given a particular risky portfolio, the risk-free asset allows us to use the Capital Allocation Line to construct an optimal complete portfolio tailored to any investor's specific risk aversion.

[width=0.8]cal_borrowing_rates.png New Topics 2.1 Diversification and Portfolio Risk Systematic Risk [width=0.8]systematic_risk.png 2.1 Diversification and Portfolio Risk Systematic and Idiosyncratic Risk A risky asset typically contains some systematic and idiosyncratic risk. Systematic Risk Also known as market risk, or undiversifiable risk. People demand compensation to hold this risk because it cannot be eliminated. The market risk premium is compensation for holding systematic risk. Idiosyncratic Risk This risk is specific to an asset. It can be ``diversified" away. This risk is not compensated because it can be easily eliminated.

[FINA3101 Lecture_5_v2] mium is compensation for holding systematic risk. Idiosyncratic Risk This risk is specific to an asset. It can be ``diversified" away. This risk is not compensated because it can be easily eliminated. 2.1 Diversification and Portfolio Risk Diversification and Idiosyncratic Risk The main idea of diversification is that idiosyncratic risk can be cancelled out. When one stock goes up, another goes down. On average, these idiosyncratic components cancel out. If these movements did not cancel out, they would be part of systematic risk. 2.1 Diversification and Portfolio Risk Systematic and Idiosyncratic Risk Suppose risky stock returns have a systematic and idiosyncratic component: R_i = R_M + _i R_M is a common systematic risk component that cannot be diversified away. Each _i is an idiosyncratic risk component. This component is uncorrelated with the systematic risk R_M. It can be eliminated through diversification. 2.1 Diversification and Portfolio Risk Individual Stock Returns and Variance Suppose the idiosyncratic components _i are i.i.d. with mean 0 and standard deviation σ.

[FINA3101 Lecture_5_v2] liminated through diversification. 2.1 Diversification and Portfolio Risk Individual Stock Returns and Variance Suppose the idiosyncratic components _i are i.i.d. with mean 0 and standard deviation σ. The expected return of any stock i is: E(R_i) &= E(R_M) + E(_i) \\ &= E(R_M) + 0 \\ &= E(R_M) The variance is: Var(R_i) &= Var(R_M) + Var(_i) + 2Cov(R_M, _i) \\ &= σ^2_M + σ^2 + 0 \\ &= σ^2_M + σ^2 2.1 Diversification and Portfolio Risk Portfolio of Two Assets (Expected Return) Suppose we invest an equal amount in stock A and stock B: R_p2 = R_M + (1)/(2)_A + (1)/(2)_B The expected return of portfolio p2 is: E(R_p2) &= E(R_M) + (1)/(2)E(_A) + (1)/(2)E(_B) \\ &= E(R_M) + 0 + 0 \\ &= E(R_M) 2.1 Diversification and Portfolio Risk Portfolio of Two Assets (Variance) Suppose we invest an equal amount in stock A and stock B. R_p2 = R_M + (1)/(2)_A + (1)/(2)_B Because all covariance terms are zero, the variance of portfolio p2 is: Var(R_p2) &= Var(R_M) + Var((1)/(2)_A) + Var((1)/(2)_B) \\ &= Var(R_M) + (1)/(4)Var(_A) + (1)/(4)Var(_B) \\ &= σ^2_M + (1)/(4)σ^2 + (1)/(4)σ^2 \\ &= σ^2_M + (1)/(2)σ^2 2.1 Diversification and Portfolio Risk Portfolio of n Assets The expected return of portfolio pn is: E(R_pn) &= E(R_M) + sum_i=1^n (1)/(n) E(_i) \\ &= E(R_M) + 0 \\ &= E(R_M) The variance is: Var(R_pn) &= σ^2_M + (σ^2)/(n) 2.1 Diversification and Portfolio Risk Important Takeaways As n , the idiosyncratic risk approaches zero.

[FINA3101 Lecture_5_v2] ^n (1)/(n) E(_i) \\ &= E(R_M) + 0 \\ &= E(R_M) The variance is: Var(R_pn) &= σ^2_M + (σ^2)/(n) 2.1 Diversification and Portfolio Risk Important Takeaways As n , the idiosyncratic risk approaches zero. Var(R_pn) = Var(R_M) + (σ^2)/(n) Diversification eliminated the idiosyncratic component of risk. Note also, more risk does not mean more reward (slide 19). E(R_i) = E(R_M) but, Var(R_i) = σ^2_M + σ^2 > σ^2_M = Var(R_M) Investors are not compensated for risk that can be eliminated through diversification. 2.2 Portfolio of Two Risky Assets Portfolio Variance Suppose we have a portfolio p with weight w_E on an equity fund and weight w_B on a bond fund. The variance of this portfolio (σ^2_p) is: Var(R_p) = w^2_E σ^2_E + w^2_B σ^2_B + 2w_E w_B Cov(R_E, R_B) The covariance is a function of its correlation: Cov(R_E, R_B) = ρ_EB σ_E σ_B 2.2 Portfolio of Two Risky Assets Correlation If ρ is less than 1, then there are benefits to diversification. By combining two risky portfolios, we can create a new risky portfolio with a higher Sharpe ratio than with the underlying portfolios. When the correlation ρ is less than one, we can build portfolios that have lower volatility and higher returns.

[FINA3101 Lecture_5_v2] te a new risky portfolio with a higher Sharpe ratio than with the underlying portfolios. When the correlation ρ is less than one, we can build portfolios that have lower volatility and higher returns. 2.2 Portfolio of Two Risky Assets Correlation Example Suppose that ρ = -1, what portfolio weights minimize the variance of the portfolio? σ^2_p &= w^2_E σ^2_E + w^2_B σ^2_B + 2w_E w_B ρ_EB σ_E σ_B \\ &= w^2_E σ^2_E + w^2_B σ^2_B - 2w_E w_B σ_E σ_B \\ &= w^2_E σ^2_E - 2w_E σ_E w_B σ_B + w^2_B σ^2_B \\ Applying\ notable\ identities: &= (w_E σ_E - w_B σ_B)^2 Thus, w_E σ_E = w_B σ_B. At this portfolio weight, variance is equal to zero. 2.2 Portfolio of Two Risky Assets Benefits from Diversification Generally, as long as ρ < 1, there will be some benefits from diversification. The minimum variance portfolio will have a variance lower than either the equity or bond fund. 2.2 Portfolio of Two Risky Assets Benefits from Diversification [width=0.6]bod12665_0705.png 2.2 Portfolio of Two Risky Assets Portfolio Opportunity Set [width=0.8]portfolio_opportunity_set.png 2.2 Portfolio of Two Risky Assets Optimal Risky Portfolio [width=0.8]Optimal_risky_portfolio.png 2.3 The Markowitz Model Question: How do you decide which stocks, bonds, and other assets to buy? 2.3 The Markowitz Model Portfolio Theory Answers This Question If we make some simple, but reasonable, assumptions about the world, we will be able to answer this question.

[FINA3101 Lecture_5_v2] s, and other assets to buy? 2.3 The Markowitz Model Portfolio Theory Answers This Question If we make some simple, but reasonable, assumptions about the world, we will be able to answer this question. Assumptions: Investors do not have private information about stocks. Stock prices are “correct.” Investors are risk averse: Investors want to make more money, but they are afraid of losing money. They will want to balance return and risk. This will lead everyone to invest in the same diversified market portfolio. 2.3 The Markowitz Model “Portfolio Selection” Markowitz (1952) This paper was written during a time of great advancements in Economics. John von Neumann's 1944 book "Theory of Games and Economic Behavior" (with Oskar Mortgenstern) is credited with formalizing utility theory and randomness, which was necessary for studying how people make decisions when faced with risk. Markowitz (1952) showed how people should invest their money when they are uninformed and care about both returns and risk. This paper forms the foundation of all of modern investing. 2.3 The Markowitz Model Who are Uninformed Investors? Markowitz’s solution was instantly applicable to any regular person or institution that wanted to invest their money. Almost everyone is uninformed. Most people do not have special information about the stock market. Professional traders (hedge funds) compete to discover and trade on information, making prices efficient.

[FINA3101 Lecture_5_v2] st everyone is uninformed. Most people do not have special information about the stock market. Professional traders (hedge funds) compete to discover and trade on information, making prices efficient. Regular people benefit from this competition among traders. Almost everyone is risk averse. Most people want to earn more money, but are afraid of losses. 2.3 The Markowitz Model Minimum Variance Portfolios By combining individual assets, investors can create a portfolio that has a lower risk (variance) than any of its individual components. [width=0.7]bod12665_0710.png 2.3 The Markowitz Model Portfolio Return and Risk For a portfolio with weights w_1, w_2, , w_n: E(r_p) = sum_i=1^n w_i E(r_i) σ_p^2 = sum_i=1^n sum_j=1^n w_i w_j Cov(r_i, r_j) Expected portfolio return is the weighted average of individual returns. Portfolio risk depends on both variances and covariances. Diversification reduces total risk unless all assets move perfectly together. 2.3 The Markowitz Model Optimize the Portfolio Goal Find the set of portfolios that: Minimize variance for a given expected return, or Maximize expected return for a given variance. Efficient Frontier The Efficient Frontier is a curve that plots all the portfolios that offer the highest possible expected return for a given level of risk.

[FINA3101 Lecture_5_v2] mize expected return for a given variance. Efficient Frontier The Efficient Frontier is a curve that plots all the portfolios that offer the highest possible expected return for a given level of risk. 2.3 The Markowitz Model The Efficient Portfolio Set [width=0.65]bod12665_0712.png 2.3 The Markowitz Model Add the Riskless Asset The riskless asset can be thought of as government bonds or the banking sector. It allows you to borrow or lend money at the rate R_0. The riskless rate gives you a positive return with zero risk. It will dominate many portfolios. Any linear combination of the riskless asset and a portfolio w will have a mean and standard deviation that lies on the line segment between the riskless asset and the portfolio. These are the blue lines in the next slide and also represent “Sharpe ratios.” 2.3 The Markowitz Model The efficient frontier of risky assets with the optimal CAL Portfolio P, the tangency point of CAL and the efficient frontier, maximizing the Sharpe ratio is the optimal risky portfolio. [width=0.65]bod12665_0713.png 2.3 The Markowitz Model The Market Portfolio \& Capital Market Line All investors have the same information and the same expectations about future returns, volatilities, and correlations for all assets. In aggregation, all investors calculate the same efficient frontier of risky assets. The combined portfolio of all investors' risky holdings must be that same portfolio.

[FINA3101 Lecture_5_v2] d correlations for all assets. In aggregation, all investors calculate the same efficient frontier of risky assets. The combined portfolio of all investors' risky holdings must be that same portfolio. This combined portfolio is, by definition, the Market Portfolio including every available stock, bond, real estate asset, etc., each weighted by its proportion of the total market value. The CAL, combining the risk-free asset and the Market Portfolio, is called the Capital Market Line (CML) 2.3 The Markowitz Model Everyone Chooses a Portfolio on the CML [width=0.8]CML.png 2.3 The Markowitz Model Risk Aversion and the CML The CML says: If you are more risk averse, you will hold a small fraction of your portfolio in w_M (y is small, close to zero). If you are less risk averse, you will hold a large fraction of your portfolio in w_M (y is large, close to one, or even larger). If y > 1, you are borrowing money to invest in the stock market. This is called leverage. 2.3 The Markowitz Model Buy the Market Portfolio Markowitz’s model was a breakthrough. It said that for most people and institutions, the best investment they can make is to invest in the market portfolio. That is, to invest in every stock in the same proportion as the total market. The weight of stock i is equal to the market value of stock i divided by the market value of the entire market.

[FINA3101 Lecture_5_v2] portfolio. That is, to invest in every stock in the same proportion as the total market. The weight of stock i is equal to the market value of stock i divided by the market value of the entire market. w_i = sum_j=1^N MV_j 2.3 The Markowitz Model Markowitz’s Solution An uninformed investor should invest in the diversified market portfolio. Regardless of risk aversion, everyone will invest some amount of money in the market portfolio. A very risk averse person may save 90\ A not very risk averse person may save 25\ Some people may even borrow money from the bank to invest in the market portfolio. This solution created a large demand for the market portfolio and led to the rise of the mutual fund industry. 2.3 The Markowitz Model The Mutual Fund Industry is Large [width=0.7]mutual_fund.png Conclusion For the vast majority of people, they should invest in the market portfolio. This is the most powerful and important result in finance, and has led to the development of the \$20 trillion passive mutual fund market. This idea has “given people what they want.”

[FINA3101 Lecture_7] m are set? This Lecture's Big Question Question: What is the fair return you should expect for investing in a risky stock? The answer is a core finance concept: the Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM). Remember our last topic? We saw that all rational investors would hold the same "best" risky portfolio (the market portfolio). CAPM's insight is simple but powerful: If everyone holds the market, the only risk that matters for an individual stock is the risk it adds to that market portfolio. This is a stock's market risk, and it's the only risk you get paid to take. The bottom line is: more market risk, more reward. The market portfolio and the tangent portfolio Recall the CAL: When a risk-free asset and N risky assets are available, the optimal portfolio choice for mean-variance investors is to combine the risk-free asset and the tangent portfolio This lecture: The starting point of the CAPM is Markowitz's key contribution, that the best thing anyone can do is invest in the diversified market portfolio.

[FINA3101 Lecture_7] risk-free asset and the tangent portfolio This lecture: The starting point of the CAPM is Markowitz's key contribution, that the best thing anyone can do is invest in the diversified market portfolio. Under certain assumptions, the broad market portfolio is the tangent portfolio Under the same assumptions, any stock’s risk premium is related to its risk level (this is how we price every asset) CAPM is: a model where the market portfolio is the tangent portfolio a model that links any stock’s risk premium to its risk contribution to the market portfolio Key Points of the Assumptions The CAPM is developed based on the set of assumptions focused on the following points: All investors are alike All mean-variance investors They only care about one period ahead All investors hold same belief on the assets’ expected return and variances The market is frictionless (borrow and lend at the risk free rate, no transaction costs) Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) With these assumptions, the market portfolio (M) is the tangent portfolio of the CAL M includes all traded assets, in our setting, stocks All stocks are held in proportional to their market capitalization (value-weighted) All investors hold a combination of risk-free asset and the market portfolio Why the market portfolio is the tangent portfolio? [FINA3101 Lecture_7] R_f))/(Var(R_m-R_f)) The Capital Asset Pricing Model (CAPM) β_i = (cov(R_i, R_M))/(σ^2(R_M)) = (Cov(R_i-R_f,R_m-R_f))/(Var(R_m-R_f)) Risk premium on an individual stock is a function of its covariance with the market The key message: beta, instead of standard deviation, is the measure for risk The phrase "more risk, more reward" refers to beta If beta is high, then the stock is highly exposed to the market, and it will earn a high return on average. If beta is low, then the stock is not very exposed to the market, and it will earn a low return on average. Example 1: Calculating the Required Rate of Return A primary use of CAPM is to determine the required rate of return for an asset—the minimum return an investor should demand for its level of risk. Scenario An investor is evaluating a tech stock, given the following market data: Risk-Free Rate (R_f): 3\ Expected Market Return (E(R_m)): 10\ This gives a Market Risk Premium of 10\ The tech stock is volatile and has a calculated Beta: Stock's Beta (β): 1.5 Example 1: Application and Decision Calculation Using the CAPM formula, we find the stock's required return: Required Return &= R_f + β (E(R_m) - R_f) \\ &= 3\ &= 3\ &= 13.5\ Conclusion The investor should not buy this stock unless they expect it to return at least 13.5\ If the expected return is 15\ If the expected return is 12\ Security market line Security market line: a plot of stocks’ betas against their risk premium [width=0.6]sml.png Three risk-return relation charts Capital Allocation Line: Combining the riskfree asset (t-bill) and one risky portfolio Under Markowitz Portfolio Theory, CAL connects T-bill and the tangent portfolio is the efficient frontier Capital Market Line: Combining the riskfree asset (t-bill) and the market portfolio Under CAPM, CML is the optimal CAL; the market portfolio is the tangent portfolio Security market line: Describe the risk premium and the beta (the risk) of individual stocks Recap: CAPM key points CAPM is an asset pricing model It relies on equilibrium conditions to determine any s

[FINA3101 Lecture_8_v2] Objective In this lecture, we will study the most important tools of finance, Arbitrage Using the Law of One Price and arbitrage we will develop a very flexible framework for pricing assets, The Arbitrage Pricing Theory Arbitrage "Arbitrage" captures the fundamental spirit of financial markets Noun: an arbitrage is a trading opportunity where we can make a "free" profit Verb: to arbitrage means to trade in a way to profit from arbitrage opportunities Arbitrage Example Suppose there are two bonds, each will payoff \100 tomorrow, and each has the same risk Suppose the price of bond A is \50 and the price of bond B is \25 The return of bond A is 100\ Does this allow for a "free" profit? Think-Pair-Share 🧠 Arbitrage Example Bond A: Price = \50, Payoff = \100 Bond B: Price = \25, Payoff = \100 (Assume same risk, same time to payoff) Question: How could you make a guaranteed, risk-free profit with \0 of your own money? What would you buy? What would you sell (short)? What is your net cash flow today and tomorrow? Arbitrage Portfolio What is a "free" profit? Consider an investment opportunity that requires no money and creates positive expected profits with no risk of loss We can form an "arbitrage portfolio" p which is a portfolio where the weights sum to 0: sum_i=0^N w_p,i = 0 Arbitrage Example We can form an arbitrage portfolio with weights w_p,A = -100\ This is a long position in Bond B and a short position in Bond A The portfolio weights sum to zero The portfolio returns are: r

[FINA3101 Lecture_8_v2] ge Example We can form an arbitrage portfolio with weights w_p,A = -100\ This is a long position in Bond B and a short position in Bond A The portfolio weights sum to zero The portfolio returns are: r_p &= w_p,Ar_A + w_p,Br_B \\ &= -1(100\ &= 200\ Arbitrage vs. Complete Portfolios Why is this valuable? We can earn a higher return with a complete portfolio w_B that invests 100\ r_B = 300\ We can earn an even higher return by adding the arbitrage portfolio to the complete portfolio This portfolio, w_B + w_p, is also a complete portfolio r_B + r_p = 500\ Arbitrage and Leverage In fact, we can add multiples of the arbitrage portfolio to our complete portfolio This will depend on how much our broker lets us short Suppose our broker allows 300\ We can form a complete portfolio: w_B + 3w_p This portfolio has returns: r_B + 3r_p = 900\ Price Pressure Arbitrage is immensely valuable and when these opportunities arise investors will try and exploit them as much as possible This is the purpose of a hedge fund Traders will buy the undervalued asset and short sell the overvalued asset This leads to price pressure Arbitrage Example: Price Pressure Recall that in our example there are two bonds, each will payoff \100 tomorrow, and each has the same risk The price of bond A is \50 and the price of bond B is \25 \[r_A = P_A,1P_A,0 - 1 = 100\ \[r_B = P_B,1P_B,0 - 1 = 300\ The price of A will decrease and the price of B will increase Arbitrage price pressure will force prices to adjust until: \
